title: Learning to Prompt for Vision-Language Models

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## LEARNING TO PROMPT FOR VISION-LANGUAGE MODELS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Vision-language pre-training has recently emerged as a promising alternative for representation learning. It shifts from the tradition of using images and discrete labels for learning a fixed set of weights, seen as visual concepts, to aligning images and raw text for two separate encoders. Such a paradigm benefits from a broader source of supervision and allows zero-shot transfer to downstream tasks since visual concepts can be diametrically generated from natural language, known as prompt. In this paper, we identify that a major challenge of deploying such models in practice is prompt engineering. This is because designing a proper prompt, especially for context words surrounding a class name, requires domain expertise and typically takes a significant amount of time for words tuning since a slight change in wording could have a huge impact on performance. Moreover, different downstream tasks require specific designs, further hampering the efficiency of deployment. To overcome this challenge, we propose a simple approach named _context optimization (CoOp)_ . The main idea is to model context in prompts using continuous representations and perform end-to-end learning from data while keeping the pre-trained parameters fixed. In this way, the design of task-relevant prompts can be fully automated. Experiments on 11 datasets show that CoOp effectively turns pre-trained vision-language models into data-efficient visual learners, requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin and able to gain significant improvements when using more shots (e.g., at 16 shots the average gain is around 17% with the highest reaching over 50%). CoOp also exhibits strong robustness to distribution shift.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The traditional approach for visual representation learning is to train vision models to predict for a fixed set of object categories using discrete labels (He et al., 2016; Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). However, this approach limits visual recognition systems to closed-set visual concepts defined during training, making them unable to handle new categories once deployed in target environments, since additional data are required for learning a new classifier. Recently, vision-language pre-training such as CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021) has emerged as a promising alternative. The main idea is to align images and raw text using two separate encoders—one for each modality. Through large-scale pre-training, vision-language models are allowed to learn open-set visual concepts and can readily be transferred to downstream tasks. In particular, for each new classification task, one can synthesize the classification weights by feeding natural language describing classes of interest to the text encoder, and compare them with image features produced by the image encoder.

We observe that for pre-trained vision-language models, the text input, known as prompt, plays a key role in downstream datasets. However, identifying the right prompt is a non-trivial task, which often takes a significant amount of time for words tuning—since a slight change in wording could make a huge difference in performance. For instance, for Caltech101 (Figure 1(a), 2nd vs. 3rd prompt), adding “a” before the class token brings more than 5% increase in accuracy. Moreover, prompt engineering also requires expertise about the task and ideally the language model’s underlying mechanism. This is exemplified in Figure 1(b-d) where adding task-relevant context can lead to significant improvements, i.e., “flower” for Flowers102, “texture” for DTD and “satellite” for EuroSAT. Tuning the sentence structure could bring further improvements, e.g., putting “a type of flower” after the class token for Flowers102, keeping only “texture” in the context for DTD, and

adding “centered” before “satellite photo” for EuroSAT. However, even with extensive tuning, the resulting prompts are by no means guaranteed to be optimal for these downstream tasks.

Inspired by recent prompt learning research in NLP (Shin et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2021), we propose _context optimization (CoOp)_[1] to automate prompt engineering to allow more efficient and task-specific transfer for pre-trained vision-language models. Specifically, we model a prompt’s context using continuous representations which are essentially initialized with random vectors with the same dimension as word embeddings (see Figure 2). The context could be shared among all classes or designed to be class-specific. During training, we simply minimize the prediction error using the cross-entropy loss with respect to the learnable context vectors, while keeping the pre-trained parameters fixed. The gradients can be back-propagated all the way through the text encoder, distilling the rich knowledge encoded in the parameters for learning task-relevant context.

To demonstrate the effectiveness of CoOp, we benchmark on 11 datasets, which cover a diverse set of visual recognition tasks including classification on generic objects, scenes, actions and fine-grained categories, as well as specialized tasks like recognizing textures and satellite imagery. The results show that CoOp can effectively turn pre-trained vision-language models into data-efficient visual learners, requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin. The performance can also be further boosted by using more shots, e.g., at 16 shots the margin over hand-crafted prompts averages at around 17% and reaches over 50% for the highest. CoOp also outperforms the linear probe alternative known as a strong few-shot learning baseline (Tian et al., 2020), and crucially, demonstrates much stronger robustness to distribution shift. Extensive analysis is also conducted to offer a comprehensive picture on how to apply CoOp in practice. The source code for reproducing the experiments will be released to facilitate future research.

## 2 METHODOLOGY

2.1 VISION-LANGUAGE PRE-TRAINING

We briefly introduce vision-language pre-training with a particular focus on CLIP (Radford et al., 2021). Our approach is applicable to broader CLIP-like vision-language models.

**Models** CLIP consists of two encoders, one for images and the other for text. The image encoder aims to map high-dimensional images into a low-dimensional embedding space. The architecture of the image encoder can take the form of a CNN like ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) or a ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2021). On the other hand, the text encoder is built on top of a Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) and aims to generate text representations from natural language.

> 1CoOp is pronounced as /ku:p/.

Figure 2: Overview of context optimization (CoOp).
Specifically, given a sequence of words (tokens), such as “a photo of a dog.”, CLIP first converts
each one of the token (including punctuation) into a lower-cased byte pair encoding (BPE) represen-
tation (Sennrich et al., 2016), which is essentially a unique numeric ID. The vocabulary size in CLIP
is 49,152. To facilitate minibatch processing, each text sequence is encompassed with the [SOS]
and [EOS] tokens and capped at a fixed length of 77. After that, the IDs are mapped to 512-D word
embedding vectors, which are then passed on to the Transformer. Finally, the features at the [EOS]
token position are layer normalized and further processed by a linear projection layer.
Training CLIP is trained to align the two embedding spaces learned for images and text respec-
tively. Specifically, the learning objective is formulated as a contrastive loss. Given a batch of
image-text pairs, CLIP maximizes the cosine similarity for matched pairs while minimizes the co-
sine similarity for all other unmatched pairs. To learn diverse visual concepts that are more trans-
ferable to downstream tasks, CLIP’s team collects a large training dataset consisting of 400 million
image-text pairs.
Zero-Shot Inference Since CLIP is pre-trained to predict whether an image matches a textual de-
scription, it naturally fits zero-shot recognition. This is achieved by comparing image features with
the classification weights synthesized by the text encoder, which takes as input textual descriptions
specifying classes of interest. Formally, let f be image features extracted by the image encoder
for an image  x  and  { w i} [K] i =1 [a set of weight vectors generated by the text encoder.] [K] [denotes the]
number of classes and each w i is derived from a prompt that could have the form of “a photo of
a [CLASS].” where the class token is replaced by the specific class name, such as “cat”, “dog” or
“car”. The prediction probability is then computed as

where _τ_ is a temperature parameter learned by CLIP and _< ·, · >_ denotes cosine similarity.

## 2.2 CONTEXT OPTIMIZATION

We propose context optimization (CoOp), which avoids manual prompt tuning by modeling context words with continuous vectors that are end-to-end learned from data. An overview is shown in Figure 2. Specifically, the prompt given to the text encoder _g_ ( _·_ ) is designed with the following form,

where each [V] _m_ ( _m ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , M }_ ) is a vector with the same dimension as word embeddings (i.e., 512 for CLIP), and _M_ is a hyperparameter specifying the number of context tokens. Note that the

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

context here is _shared_ among all classes, which is called unified context and different from classspecific context that is introduced later.
By forwarding a prompt _**t**_ to the text encoder _g_ ( _·_ ), we can obtain a classification weight vector representing a visual concept. The prediction probability is computed as
where the class token within each prompt _**t** i_ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the _i_ -th class name.

Training is performed to minimize the standard classification loss based on the cross-entropy, and the gradients can be back-propagated all the way through the text encoder _g_ ( _·_ ), making use of the rich knowledge encoded in the parameters to optimize the context. The design of continuous representations also allows full exploration in the word embedding space, which facilitates the learning of task-relevant context.
**Other Variants** Other than placing the class token at the end of a sequence as in Equation (2), we can also put it in the middle like

which increases flexibility for learning—theoretically, the prompt is allowed to either fill the latter cells with supplementary descriptions or cut off the sentence earlier by using a termination signal such as full stop.

Another option is to design class-specific context (CSC) where context vectors are independent to each class, i.e., [V]1 _[i]_[[][V][]] _[i]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[i] M_[= [][V][]] _[j]_ 1[[][V][]] _[j]_ 2 _[. . .]_[ [][V][]] _[j] M_[for] _[ i]_[=] _[j]_[and] _[ i, j][∈{]_[1] _[, . . . , K][}]_[.][As an alter-] native to unified context, we find that CSC is particularly useful for some fine-grained classification tasks.

## 3 EXPERIMENTS

## 3.1 FEW-SHOT LEARNING

**Datasets** We select 11 publicly available image classification datasets used in CLIP: ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009), Caltech101 (Fei-Fei et al., 2004), OxfordPets (Parkhi et al., 2012), StanfordCars (Krause et al., 2013), Flowers102 (Nilsback & Zisserman, 2008), Food101 (Bossard et al., 2014), FGVCAircraft (Maji et al., 2013), SUN397 (Xiao et al., 2010), DTD (Cimpoi et al., 2014), EuroSAT (Helber et al., 2019) and UCF101 (Soomro et al., 2012) (see Appendix A for their statistics). These datasets constitute a comprehensive benchmark, which covers a diverse set of vision tasks including classification on generic objects, scenes, actions and fine-grained categories, as well as specialized tasks like recognizing textures and satellite imagery. We follow the few-shot evaluation protocol adopted in CLIP (Radford et al., 2021), using 1, 2, 4, 8 and 16 shots for training respectively and deploying models in the full test sets. The average results over three runs are reported for comparison.

**Training Details** CoOp has four versions: positioning the class token in the end or middle; unified context vs. CSC. Unless otherwise stated, ResNet-50 (He et al., 2016) is used as the image encoder’s backbone and the number of context tokens _M_ is set to 16. Investigations on other design choices are discussed in Section 3.3. All models are built on top of the open-sourced CLIP.[2] CoOp’s context vectors are randomly initialized by drawing from a zero-mean Gaussian distribution with standard deviation equal to 0.02. Training is done with SGD and an initial learning rate of 0.002, which is decayed by the cosine annealing rule. The maximum epoch is set to 200 for 16/8 shots, 100 for 4/2 shots, and 50 for 1 shot (except for ImageNet where the maximum epoch is fixed to 50). To mitigate explosive gradients observed in the early training iterations, we use the warmup trick by fixing the learning rate to 1 _e−_ 5 during the first epoch.

> 2 https://github.com/openai/CLIP.

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 3: **Main results of few-shot learning on the 11 datasets** . Overall, CoOp effectively turns CLIP into a strong few-shot learner (solid lines), achieving significant improvements over zero-shot CLIP (stars) and performing favorably against the linear probe alternative (dashed lines). _M_ denotes the context length. “end” or “mid” means putting the class token in the end or middle. CSC means class-specific context.

**Baseline Methods** We compare CoOp with two baseline methods. The first is zero-shot CLIP, which is based on hand-crafted prompts. We follow the guideline of prompt engineering introduced by Radford et al. (2021). For generic objects and scenes, “a photo of a [CLASS].” is adopted. For fine-grained categories, task-relevant context is added like “a type of pet” for OxfordPets and “a type of food” for Food101. When it comes to specialized tasks such as recognizing textures in DTD, the prompt is customized as “[CLASS] texture.” where the class names are adjectives like “bubbly” and “dotted”. See Appendix A for the details. The second baseline is linear probe CLIP. As suggested by Radford et al. (2021) and a recent study on few-shot learning (Tian et al., 2020), training a linear classifier on top of high-quality pre-trained models’ features (like CLIP) can easily achieve performance that is on a par with that of state-of-the-art few-shot learning methods, which are often much more sophisticated. We follow the same training method used by Radford et al. (2021) to train linear probe CLIP.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

**Comparison with Hand-Crafted Prompts** Figure 3 summarizes the results. Our default model is CLIP+CoOp with the class token positioned in the end. The two different ways of positioning the class token achieve similar performance as their curves highly overlap. From the average performance displayed in the top-left corner, we observe that CLIP+CoOp is a strong few-shot learner, requiring only two shots on average to obtain a decent margin over zero-shot CLIP. Given 16 shots for training, the average gap brought by CoOp can be further increased to around 17%.

Figure 4 ranks the absolute improvements obtained by CoOp at 16 shots over hand-crafted prompts. Huge improvements are observed on specialized tasks namely EuroSAT and DTD where the increase in performance reaches over 50% and 20% respectively. The jumps in performance are also significant (those more than 10%) on most fine-grained datasets including Flowers102, StanfordCars and FGVCAircraft, as well as on scene and action recognition datasets (SUN397 & UCF101). Since ImageNet is a challenging dataset that contains 1,000 classes, the 5.05% improvement is also noteworthy. In contrast, the increases on the two fine-grained datasets, OxfordPets and Food101, are less appealing. By digging into CLIP+CoOp’s curves on these two datasets in Figure 3, we find there is a loss of momentum in performance improvements even with more shots used, seemingly an overfitting problem. A potential solution is to impose higher regularizations like increasing the weight decay. Nonetheless, the overall results are strong enough to serve as evidence of CoOp’s capability of learning task-relevant prompts in a data-efficient way.

**Comparison with Linear Probe CLIP** In terms of the overall performance (Figure 3, topleft), CLIP+CoOp demonstrates clear advantages over linear probe CLIP. The latter requires 4 shots on average to match the zero-shot’s performance while CoOp’s average gains at 4 shots are already more than 10%. It is also clear that the gaps in the extreme low-data regime such as one or two shots are much larger, suggesting that CoOp is much more effective than learning a linear classifier from scratch for fewshot learning. We also observe that linear probe CLIP is comparable to CLIP+CoOp on the two specialized tasks (DTD & EuroSAT) as well as on a couple of fine-grained datasets (Flowers102 & FGVCAircraft)—this is not too surprising as the pre-trained CLIP space has been

proved powerful, making the linear probe model a strong competitor. Nevertheless, CoOp’s CSC version can beat linear probe CLIP on the aforementioned datasets, and moreover, shows much better potential when more shots become available.

**Unified vs. Class-Specific Context** On average, using unified context leads to better performance. In terms of when to apply CSC and when not to, we have the following suggestions. For generic objects (ImageNet & Caltech101), scenes (SUN397) and actions (UCF101), using unified context is clearly better. Unified context also works better on some fine-grained datasets including OxfordPets and Food101, but on others like StanfordCars, Flowers102 and FGVCAircraft the CSC version is preferred. CSC also yields better performance on the two specialized tasks, DTD and EuroSAT, at 16 shots in particular. However, CSC mostly underperforms unified context in challenging low-data scenarios (fewer than 8 shots), which makes sense because CSC has more parameters than unified context and needs more data for training.

## 3.2 ROBUSTNESS TO DISTRIBUTION SHIFT

Since CoOp requires training on a specific data distribution, it risks learning spurious correlations that are detrimental to generalization in unseen distributions (domains), as suggested in recent studies (Taori et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2021). On the contrary, zero-shot CLIP is not tied to a specific data distribution and has exhibited strong robustness to distribution shift (Radford et al., 2021). In this section, we aim to unveil how robust CoOp is to distribution shift, in comparison to zero-shot CLIP and the linear probe model.

**Datasets** The source dataset is ImageNet. The target datasets are ImageNetV2 (Recht et al., 2019), ImageNet-Sketch (Wang et al., 2019), ImageNet-A (Hendrycks et al., 2021b) and ImageNetR (Hendrycks et al., 2021a), all of which have compatible class names with ImageNet allowing seamless transfer for the prompts learned by CoOp. ImageNetV2 is a reproduced test set using different sources while following ImageNet’s data collection process. ImageNet-Sketch contains sketch images belonging to the same 1,000 ImageNet classes. Both ImageNet-A and -R contain 200 classes derived from a subset of ImageNet’s 1,000 classes. The former consists of real-world adversarially filtered images that cause current ImageNet classifiers to produce low results, whereas the latter features a rendition of the ImageNet classes in diverse image styles such as paintings, cartoons and sculptures.

**Results** Table 1 summarizes the results. It is surprising that CLIP+CoOp exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset. This suggests that the learned prompts are also generalizable. Moreover, it is interesting to see that using fewer context tokens leads to better robustness. More results with different vision backbones are provided in Appendix B.1 where the conclusion remains the same. In contrast, linear probe CLIP obtains much worse results on these target datasets, exposing its weakness in domain generalization.

## 3.3 FURTHER ANALYSIS

**Comparison with Prompt Ensembling** Radford et al. (2021) have suggested that additional improvements can be obtained by ensembling over multiple zero-shot classifiers generated using different hand-crafted prompts, such as “a photo of the large [CLASS].”, “a bad photo of the [CLASS].” and “a origami [CLASS].”, which reflect a different scale, view and abstraction respectively for an image. We are interested to know whether the prompts learned by CoOp can still maintain advantages when compared with prompt ensembling. For fair comparison, we use the select prompts from Radford et al. (2021), which have been extensively tuned on ImageNet, to construct the ensemble classifier. Table 2 presents the results of prompt engineering (i.e., using a single hand-crafted prompt), prompt ensembling and CoOp, confirming that CoOp is still the best performing method. Additional results are provided in Appendix B.2 to show that CoOp also beats prompt ensembling when more advanced vision backbones are used. Given the potential of prompt ensembling, future work could investigate how to improve CoOp from the ensembling perspective.

**Context Length** How many context tokens should be used? And is it better to have more context tokens? The results in Section 3.2 suggest having shorter context length benefits domain generalization. Here we study this hyperparameter for source datasets. Specifically, we repeat experiments on the 11 datasets by varying the context length from 4 to 8 to 16. The average results are shown in Figure 5(a), which indicate that having more context tokens leads to better performance and that positioning the class token in the middle gains more momentum with longer context length. To sum

**----- Start of picture text -----**

(a) Context length (b) Vision backbones
**----- End of picture text -----**

Figure 5: Investigations on CoOp’s context length and various vision backbones.

up, there is no golden rule for selecting perfect context length since one needs to balance between performance and robustness to distribution shift. See Appendix B.3 for the dataset-specific results.

**Vision Backbones** Figure 5(b) summarizes the results on the 11 datasets using a variety of vision backbones covering both CNNs and ViTs. The results are expected: the more advanced the backbone, the better the performance. The gap between CoOp and hand-crafted prompts is significant across all architectures. See Appendix B.4 for the dataset-specific results.

**Initialization** We compare random initialization with manual initialization. The latter uses the embeddings of “a photo of a” to initialize the context vectors for the 11 datasets. For fair comparison, we also set the context length to 4 when using random initialization. Table 3 suggests a “good” initialization only brings a small improvement. Though further tuning of the initialization words might help, in practice we suggest using the simple random initialization method.

**Interpreting the Learned Prompts** is difficult because the context vectors are optimized in a continuous space. We resort to an indirect way by searching within the vocabulary for words that are closest to the learned vectors based on the Euclidean distance. Note that CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) uses the BPE representation (Sennrich et al., 2016) for tokenization, so the vocabulary includes subwords that frequently appear in text, such as “hu” (subsumed by many words like “hug” and “human”). Table 4 shows the searched results on some datasets. We observe that a few words are somewhat relevant to the tasks, such as “enjoyed” for Food101, “fluffy” and “paw” for OxfordPets, and “pretty” for DTD. But when connecting all the nearest words together, the prompts do not make much sense. We also observe that when using manual initialization (like “a photo of a”), the nearest words for the converged vectors are mostly the ones used for initialization. We conjecture that the learned vectors might encode meanings that are beyond the existing vocabulary. Overall, we are unable to draw any firm conclusion based on the observations because using nearest words to interpret the learned prompts could be inaccurate—the semantics of the vectors is not necessarily correlated with the nearest words.

## 4 RELATED WORK

**Vision-Language Models** have recently demonstrated great potential in learning generic visual representations and allowing zero-shot transfer to a variety of downstream classification tasks (Radford et al., 2021; Jia et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020). To our knowledge, the recent developments in vision-language learning, particularly CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) and ALIGN (Jia et al., 2021), are largely driven by advances in the following three areas: i) text representation learning with Transformers (Vaswani et al., 2017), ii) large-minibatch contrastive representation learning (Chen et al., 2020; He et al., 2020; H´enaff et al., 2020), and iii) web-scale training datasets—CLIP benefits from 400 million curated image-text pairs while ALIGN exploits 1.8 billion noisy image-text pairs.
The idea of mapping images and text onto a common embedding space has been studied since nearly a decade ago (Socher et al., 2013; Frome et al., 2013; Elhoseiny et al., 2013), but with drastically

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Table 4: The nearest words for each of the 16 context vectors learned by CoOp, with their distances shown in parentheses. N/A means non-Latin characters.

different technologies. For text features extraction, early work has mainly utilized pre-trained word vectors (Socher et al., 2013; Frome et al., 2013) or the hand-crafted TF-IDF features (Elhoseiny et al., 2013; Lei Ba et al., 2015). Matching images and text features has been formulated as metric learning (Frome et al., 2013), multi-label classification (Joulin et al., 2016; Gomez et al., 2017), n-gram language learning (Li et al., 2017), and the recently proposed captioning (Desai & Johnson, 2021). Our work is orthogonal to recent research in vision-language models, aiming to facilitate the deployment of such models in downstream datasets.

**Prompt Learning in NLP** Knowledge probing for large pre-trained language models, formally defined by Petroni et al. (2019) as “fill-in-the-blank” cloze tests, has recently sparked interest in prompt learning research in NLP (Shin et al., 2020; Jiang et al., 2020; Li & Liang, 2021; Zhong et al., 2021; Lester et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021b). The basic idea of knowledge probing is to induce pre-trained language models to generate answers given cloze-style prompts, which can benefit a number of downstream tasks, such as sentiment analysis. Jiang et al. (2020) propose to generate candidate prompts through text mining and paraphrasing, and identify the optimal ones that give the highest training accuracy. Shin et al. (2020) introduce a gradient-based approach, which searches for tokens with the largest gradient changes in the label likelihood. Most related to our work are continuous prompt learning methods (Zhong et al., 2021; Li & Liang, 2021; Lester et al., 2021) which optimize continuous vectors in the word embedding space. A drawback of such methods compared to searching discrete tokens is the lack of a clear way to visualize what “words” are learned for the vectors. We refer readers to Liu et al. (2021a) for a comprehensive survey in the topic of prompt learning in NLP.

## 5 CONCLUSION

We present CoOp, a differentiable approach that focuses on continuous prompt learning to facilitate the deployment of pre-trained vision-language models in downstream datasets. The results on the 11 datasets serve as strong evidence of CoOp’s effectiveness in data-efficient learning. The learned prompts are proved much more task-relevant than hand-crafted prompts reflected by the huge improvements in performance, as well as stronger in robustness to distribution shift. In terms of limitation, CoOp requires explicit access to the pre-trained model parameters, which might be unavailable when only the APIs of pre-trained models are provided. An interesting future direction is thus to investigate “black-box” prompt learning.

- Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, and Jian Sun. Deep residual learning for image recognition. In _CVPR_ , 2016.
Kaiming He, Haoqi Fan, Yuxin Wu, Saining Xie, and Ross Girshick. Momentum contrast for unsupervised visual representation learning. In _CVPR_ , 2020.

- Patrick Helber, Benjamin Bischke, Andreas Dengel, and Damian Borth. Eurosat: A novel dataset and deep learning benchmark for land use and land cover classification. _IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing_ , 2019.

- Olivier J. H´enaff, Aravind Srinivas, Jeffrey De Fauw, Ali Razavi, Carl Doersch, S. M. Ali Eslami, and A¨aron van den Oord. Data-efficient image recognition with contrastive predictive coding. In _ICML_ , 2020.

- Dan Hendrycks, Steven Basart, Norman Mu, Saurav Kadavath, Frank Wang, Evan Dorundo, Rahul Desai, Tyler Zhu, Samyak Parajuli, Mike Guo, Dawn Song, Jacob Steinhardt, and Justin Gilmer. The many faces of robustness: A critical analysis of out-of-distribution generalization. _ICCV_ , 2021a.
- Dan Hendrycks, Kevin Zhao, Steven Basart, Jacob Steinhardt, and Dawn Song. Natural adversarial examples. In _CVPR_ , 2021b.

- Chao Jia, Yinfei Yang, Ye Xia, Yi-Ting Chen, Zarana Parekh, Hieu Pham, Quoc V Le, Yunhsuan Sung, Zhen Li, and Tom Duerig. Scaling up visual and vision-language representation learning with noisy text supervision. In _ICML_ , 2021.

Zhengbao Jiang, Frank F Xu, Jun Araki, and Graham Neubig. How can we know what language models know? _ACL_ , 2020.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces Context Optimization (CoOp), a method for learning continuous prompt tokens to adapt pre-trained vision-language models like CLIP to downstream classification tasks. By replacing hand-crafted prompts with learnable vectors, the authors demonstrate significant performance gains in few-shot settings across 11 datasets.

The paper provides a clean, effective, and well-executed approach to prompt tuning for vision-language models. The empirical results are strong and the methodology is straightforward, making it a significant contribution to the field of efficient model adaptation.

Overview issues:

- Overfitting to Training Distributions: The authors acknowledge that CoOp performs worse than zero-shot CLIP on certain datasets when given more training data, suggesting an overfitting problem. While the paper mentions that regularization might help, it lacks a systematic investigation into how the learned prompts generalize to unseen categories or domains beyond the specific training set. The current evaluation focuses heavily on the source domain, which masks the potential for the model to memorize the specific training samples rather than learning a generalized visual concept. A more rigorous evaluation using cross-dataset transfer or held-out class sets would better demonstrate the robustness of the learned prompts.

- Lack of Interpretability for Learned Prompts: In Section 3.3, the authors attempt to interpret the learned prompts by searching for the nearest words in the vocabulary, but they correctly note that this yields incoherent results. This highlights a fundamental limitation: the learned continuous vectors exist in a latent space that does not map cleanly to natural language. The paper would benefit from a more formal analysis of what these prompts actually capture, perhaps by visualizing the attention maps of the text encoder or conducting an ablation on the prompt length's effect on semantic alignment. Without this, the 'prompt' remains a black-box parameter set rather than a truly interpretable engineering tool.

- Insufficient Analysis of Prompt Initialization: Section 3.3 briefly compares random initialization with 'a photo of a', but the analysis is limited to a single case. Given that prompt tuning is sensitive to the starting point, especially in low-data regimes, a more comprehensive study on how different initialization strategies (e.g., using task-specific text or different prompt lengths) affect convergence and final accuracy is needed. The current results suggest that random initialization is sufficient, but this may not hold across all dataset types or model backbones. Providing a more detailed sensitivity analysis would strengthen the claim that CoOp is a general-purpose solution.

- Impact on Pre-trained Feature Alignment: The paper assumes that keeping the CLIP backbone frozen is optimal, but it does not explore how the learned prompts interact with the existing alignment between the image and text encoders. Since the text encoder is fixed, the prompt optimization is essentially forcing the text features to align with the image features of the training set. It is unclear if this process degrades the model's ability to represent concepts that were not part of the training distribution. A discussion or experiment regarding the trade-off between task-specific performance and the preservation of the original pre-trained alignment would clarify the long-term utility of the method.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for hyperparameter stability: The paper relies on a fixed set of hyperparameters, such as learning rate and weight decay, across 11 diverse datasets. Because prompt optimization is notoriously sensitive to these settings—especially in extreme low-data regimes like 1-shot learning—the current results may not be representative of the method's true potential. To address this, the authors should provide a sensitivity analysis or a grid search result for at least one representative dataset (e.g., ImageNet or Flowers102) showing how the final accuracy varies with respect to the initial learning rate and the weight decay coefficient. This would confirm that the performance gains are not merely the result of fortunate hyperparameter selection.

- Absence of cross-task generalization evaluation: The paper claims CoOp enables task-specific transfer, yet it only evaluates performance on the same dataset used for training. A key test of whether the model has learned a 'prompt' or simply overfitted to the training labels is to evaluate the learned context vectors on a held-out set of classes within the same domain. I suggest the authors perform a cross-class generalization experiment—training on a subset of ImageNet classes and testing on the remaining unseen classes—to demonstrate that the learned continuous vectors capture generalizable visual-semantic knowledge rather than just memorizing the specific class-to-image mappings of the training set.

- Missing computational efficiency and latency analysis: While the paper positions CoOp as a practical deployment tool, it provides no information on the computational cost of the optimization process compared to traditional fine-tuning or zero-shot inference. Readers need to know the training time required to converge on the 16-shot setting for a standard backbone like ResNet-50. Providing a table that compares the wall-clock training time, memory footprint, and subsequent inference latency for CoOp versus a linear probe baseline would be essential for practitioners deciding whether to adopt this method in real-world deployment scenarios.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Perform a cross-dataset generalization experiment to test if prompts learned on one dataset transfer to others, addressing the overfitting concern.

- Expand the sensitivity analysis of initialization strategies to include more diverse starting points beyond 'a photo of a'.

- Include a discussion on the trade-off between task-specific accuracy and the preservation of the original pre-trained alignment, perhaps by evaluating zero-shot performance on a held-out set of tasks after prompt tuning.

comments.title: Inconsistency in CoOp performance claims across tables
comments.quote: s Table 1 summarizes the results. It is surprising that  $\mathrm{CLIP + CoOp}$  exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP to distribution shift, despite exposure to the source dataset. This suggests that the learned prompts are also generalizable. Moreover
comments.explanation: The claim of superior robustness is contradicted by the data in Table 1 for the ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R datasets. Specifically, Zero-Shot CLIP achieves 31.67% on ImageNet-Sketch, while CLIP + CoOp (M=16) achieves only 31.14%. Similarly, on ImageNet-R, Zero-Shot CLIP achieves 53.45%, whereas CLIP + CoOp (M=16) achieves 53.31%. Please qualify the claim of 'stronger robustness' by noting that this trend does not hold consistently across all distribution shift benchmarks.

comments.title: Inconsistency in training duration for ImageNet
comments.quote: The maximum epoch is set to 200 for 16/8 shots, 100 for 4/2 shots, and 50 for 1 shot (except for ImageNet where the maximum epoch is fixed to 50).
comments.explanation: The phrasing implies that for ImageNet, the maximum epoch is fixed to 50 regardless of the shot count. However, the preceding clause specifies 200, 100, and 50 epochs for 16/8, 4/2, and 1 shot respectively. Please clarify if the 50-epoch limit applies to all shot counts on ImageNet or only the 1-shot case, as the current phrasing is logically inconsistent.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim regarding performance on fine-grained datasets
comments.quote: also clear that the gaps in the extreme low-data regime such as one or two shots are much larger, suggesting that CoOp is much more effective than learning a linear classifier from scratch for few-shot learning. We also observe that linear probe CLIP is comparable to  $\mathrm{CLIP + CoOp}$  on the two specialized tasks (DTD & EuroSAT) as well as on a couple of fine-grained datasets (Flowers102 & FGVCAircr
comments.explanation: The text claims that linear probe CLIP is comparable to CoOp on Flowers102 and FGVCAircraft, but the preceding paragraph lists these as datasets where CoOp shows significant performance jumps of more than 10%. If CoOp provides a 10% gain over zero-shot CLIP, and linear probe CLIP is only 'comparable' to zero-shot CLIP in the low-data regime, then CoOp cannot be comparable to the linear probe. Please reconcile this contradiction.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding prompt relevance
comments.quote: The learned prompts are proved much more task-relevant than hand-crafted prompts reflected by the huge improvements in performance, as well as stronger in robustness to distribution shift.
comments.explanation: The claim that the prompts are 'proved' to be 'more task-relevant' is an overstatement. The paper demonstrates higher accuracy and improved robustness, but these are empirical metrics, not a formal proof of semantic task relevance. As noted in Section 3.3, interpreting the learned vectors is difficult and they do not necessarily correlate with meaningful linguistic concepts. Please rephrase this to state that the learned prompts demonstrate superior task performance rather than claiming they are 'proved' to be more 'task-relevant'.

comments.title: Ambiguity in unified context notation
comments.quote: where the class token within each prompt $\bm{t}_{i}$ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the $i$-th class name.
comments.explanation: The notation $\bm{t}_{i}$ in Equation (3) conflicts with the definition of $\bm{t}$ as a unified context in Equation (2). While the context is shared, the subscript $i$ on $\bm{t}$ suggests that the entire prompt varies by class, which is not the case for unified context. Please replace $\bm{t}_{i}$ in Equation (3) with $\bm{t} = [\text{V}]_{1} \ldots [\text{V}]_{M} [\text{CLASS}_{i}]$ to explicitly show that only the class token component is class-dependent.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces CoOp, a method that learns continuous context vectors to automate prompt engineering for vision-language models. The experiments demonstrate clear few-shot performance gains over zero-shot CLIP and linear probing across 11 datasets. The work addresses a practical bottleneck in deploying multimodal models, though the evaluation and claims require stronger statistical grounding and broader context.

The paper introduces CoOp, a straightforward yet effective method for automating prompt engineering in vision-language models by learning continuous context vectors. The experimental evaluation across 11 datasets demonstrates clear performance gains over zero-shot baselines and linear probing in few-shot regimes. The focus on data efficiency and distribution shift robustness addresses a timely need in deploying pre-trained multimodal models.

Overview issues:

- Missing statistical variance and rigorous evaluation protocol: The paper reports average results over three runs but omits standard deviations or confidence intervals in all tables and figures. Few-shot learning is notoriously sensitive to random seed selection and class permutations. Without variance metrics, readers cannot assess whether the reported margins are statistically significant or driven by favorable splits. This omission weakens the reproducibility of the findings and makes it difficult to compare CoOp against other methods that typically report variance. The revision must include standard deviation bars in Figure 3 and 4, and explicitly state the random seeds or class permutations used. A proper statistical test or at least variance reporting is necessary to validate the claimed performance gains.

- Overstated robustness claims ignore the base-to-novel class generalization gap: The authors claim CoOp exhibits strong robustness to distribution shift, even outperforming zero-shot CLIP on several ImageNet variants. The robustness evaluation only tests on datasets with compatible class names, completely sidestepping the open-set or novel-class generalization problem. As subsequent work has shown, continuous prompt tuning severely overfits to base classes, leading to poor transfer to unseen categories. By focusing solely on closed-set distribution shifts, the paper presents an incomplete picture of real-world robustness. The claim needs to be tempered to reflect that robustness is limited to the trained label space. The authors should add experiments on novel-class detection or open-set recognition to quantify how CoOp handles out-of-distribution classes, and explicitly discuss this limitation.

- Interpretability analysis fails to validate the automated prompt engineering premise: A core motivation of CoOp is automating prompt design, yet the paper admits that interpreting the learned continuous vectors is difficult. The nearest-word search yields fragmented subwords that do not form coherent phrases, and the authors concede they cannot draw firm conclusions. If the optimized prompts cannot be mapped to meaningful language, practitioners lose the ability to debug, trust, or manually refine the models. This directly compromises the practical utility of the method. The revision should incorporate a more rigorous interpretability analysis, such as probing the prompt vectors with linear classifiers or using attention visualization, rather than relying on a superficial vocabulary lookup. Alternatively, the authors should clearly frame the method as a black-box optimizer and adjust the claims accordingly.

- Optimization dynamics and training stability lack sufficient analysis: The methodology relies on backpropagating gradients through a frozen text encoder to update continuous context vectors. The authors mention using a warmup trick to mitigate explosive gradients but provide no further analysis of the optimization landscape. Continuous prompt tuning is known to be sensitive to learning rates, initialization scales, and context length, often leading to training instability or vanishing gradients. Without examining gradient norms, convergence trajectories, or sensitivity to hyperparameters, the paper leaves a critical practical gap. Readers will wonder why certain configurations fail or how to scale the method to longer contexts. The authors should include a training dynamics analysis, such as plotting gradient norms over epochs or providing a sensitivity study on the learning rate and initialization variance.

- Evaluation lacks comparison to other parameter-efficient adaptation methods: The experimental setup only compares CoOp against zero-shot CLIP and linear probe CLIP. While linear probing is a strong baseline, the field has rapidly evolved to include other parameter-efficient techniques like adapters, low-rank adaptation, and conditional prompt tuning. By restricting the comparison, the paper overstates CoOp's standing as a general-purpose adaptation method. It also misses the opportunity to contextualize CoOp's performance relative to methods that explicitly address its known weaknesses, such as overfitting to few-shot data. The revision should add comparisons to contemporaneous prompt-tuning variants or other lightweight adaptation strategies. Even if space is limited, a concise comparison table would ground the claims and clarify CoOp's specific niche.

- Worked special case for continuous prompt optimization: The paper lacks a worked special case that isolates how continuous vectors interact with the frozen text encoder's geometry. This omission matters because large-scale benchmarks obscure the underlying optimization mechanism and leave readers guessing whether the method learns meaningful features or just memorizes labels. A natural question is whether the gradients actually push the context vectors toward semantically useful regions of the embedding space. The authors should compute the learned context trajectories for a simple, fully-specified 2-class toy problem. Running CoOp on a synthetic dataset with two distinct visual concepts and plotting the vector path in the 512-D embedding space would let readers calibrate their intuitions about the method's behavior.

- Missing computational cost and memory analysis: The manuscript claims CoOp is a parameter-efficient adaptation technique but never quantifies its computational footprint. This gap is critical for publishability because practitioners cannot verify whether the method actually saves resources compared to linear probing or full fine-tuning. The context length M directly scales the trainable parameters and VRAM overhead, yet the paper provides no concrete metrics. The authors must add a table reporting the exact number of trainable parameters, training FLOPs, and inference latency for M=4, 8, and 16. Providing these implementation details would ground the efficiency claims and help readers assess deployment feasibility.

- Demonstration of failure modes under data scarcity: The paper acknowledges performance drops on OxfordPets and Food101 but fails to demonstrate exactly when and why the method breaks down. This matters because claims of data efficiency require clear boundary conditions to be useful in practice. Readers will wonder whether the collapse stems from the continuous parameterization being too expressive or from poor initialization. The authors should construct a controlled experiment varying the shot count and class overlap to identify the precise threshold where CoOp starts memorizing training labels. Showing a concrete DGP where the optimization diverges or plateaus would validate the limits of the approach and guide regularization choices.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents a solid method but suffers from incomplete evaluation protocols, overstated robustness claims, and a lack of comparative baselines. Addressing the statistical reporting gaps, expanding the robustness analysis to novel classes, and contextualizing the method against other parameter-efficient techniques are necessary before publication.

Revision targets:

- Report standard deviations and confidence intervals for all few-shot experiments, and specify the random seeds or class permutations used.

- Add experiments evaluating base-to-novel class generalization and open-set recognition to properly qualify the robustness claims.

- Conduct a deeper interpretability analysis of the learned continuous prompts, such as attention visualization or probing tasks, rather than relying on nearest-word searches.

- Include a training dynamics analysis examining gradient norms, convergence behavior, and sensitivity to initialization and learning rate.

- Compare CoOp against other parameter-efficient adaptation methods and prompt-tuning variants to contextualize its performance and limitations.

comments.title: Overstated language and robustness scope
comments.quote: The results on the 11 datasets serve as strong evidence of CoOp's effectiveness in data-efficient learning. The learned prompts are proved much more task-relevant than hand-crafted prompts reflected by the huge improvements in performance, as well as stronger in robustness to distribution shift.
comments.explanation: The conclusion uses the word "proved," which mischaracterizes empirical findings as mathematical certainty. This phrasing clashes directly with Section 3.3, where the authors admit they cannot draw firm conclusions about the semantics of the learned vectors. Performance gains show practical utility, but they do not establish a formal proof of task-relevance. Furthermore, the robustness claim implies broad generalization, yet the evaluation only covers closed-set distribution shifts. Readers will interpret this as a guarantee for out-of-distribution classes, which the paper does not test. Tone down "proved" to "demonstrate" and explicitly bound the robustness claim to the evaluated datasets.

comments.title: Missing batch size for learning rate schedule
comments.quote: Training is done with SGD and an initial learning rate of 0.002, which is decayed by the cosine annealing rule. The maximum epoch is set to 200 for 16/8 shots, 100 for 4/2 shots, and 50 for 1 shot (except for ImageNet where the maximum epoch is fixed to 50). To mitigate explosive gradients observed in the early training iterations, we use the warmup trick by fixing the learning rate to $1e-5$ during the first epoch.
comments.explanation: The optimization setup is under-specified for reproduction. Cosine annealing requires the total number of steps, which depends on the batch size. The paper never states the batch size. Additionally, the warmup schedule interrupts the cosine decay, but the text does not explain how the schedule resumes after the first epoch. Does it restart from 0.002, or continue from the warmup point? Provide the batch size and clarify the resumption rule so readers can exactly replicate the training trajectory.

comments.title: Misleading terminology for text encoder outputs
comments.quote: This is achieved by comparing image features with the classification weights synthesized by the text encoder, which takes as input textual descriptions specifying classes of interest.
comments.explanation: Referring to the text encoder outputs as "classification weights" is confusing. These are frozen text embeddings or class prototypes, not trainable weight matrices. The phrasing suggests the text encoder might be fine-tuned or that a separate head is involved. Replace "classification weights" with "textual class prototypes" to accurately reflect the frozen encoder's role.

comments.title: Undefined notation for class-specific prompts
comments.quote:
ere the class token within each prompt $\bm{t}_{i}$ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of the $i$-th class name.

Training
comments.explanation: Equation (3) introduces $ m{t}_i$ without defining it. Equation (2) defines a generic prompt $ m{t}$, but the switch to $ m{t}_i$ creates ambiguity about whether it represents the prompt before or after class replacement. Add an explicit definition for $ m{t}_i$ right after Equation (2) to close this gap.

comments.title: Transformer truncation misconception
comments.quote: which increases flexibility for learning—theoretically, the prompt is allowed to either fill the latter cells with supplementary descriptions or cut off the sentence earlier by using a termination signal such as full stop.
comments.explanation: The claim that a full stop can "cut off the sentence earlier" misunderstands how transformers process sequences. Standard self-attention applies across the entire sequence; punctuation does not natively truncate computation. All tokens contribute to the final representation unless explicit masking is used. Clarify that the termination signal modulates attention rather than stopping processing.

comments.title: Absolute vs. relative improvement metrics
comments.quote: Figure 4 ranks the absolute improvements obtained by CoOp at 16 shots over hand-crafted prompts. Huge improvements are observed on specialized tasks namely EuroSAT and DTD where the increase in performance reaches over  $50\%$  and  $20\%$  respectively.
comments.explanation: The text says "absolute improvements" but uses the "%" symbol, which conventionally denotes relative change. In few-shot learning, gains are typically measured in percentage points. If EuroSAT jumps from 40% to 90%, that is a 50 percentage point increase, not a 50% relative increase. Specify "percentage points" to prevent readers from misinterpreting the magnitude of the gains.

comments.title: Baseline ambiguity for reported gains
comments.quote: The latter requires 4 shots on average to match the zero-shot's performance while CoOp's average gains at 4 shots are already more than  $10\%$ .
comments.explanation: The phrase "CoOp's average gains at 4 shots" lacks an explicit reference point. The preceding clause compares CoOp to linear probe CLIP, so readers might assume the 10% gain is over the linear probe. Rewrite to explicitly state "CoOp's average gains over zero-shot CLIP at 4 shots are already more than 10%." This small addition removes ambiguity and aligns the comparison with the preceding clause.

comments.title: Table 2 omits context length for CoOp
comments.quote:
Table 2: Comparison with prompt ensembling.

|   | ImageNet  |
| --- | --- |
|  Prompt engineering | 55.41  |
|  Prompt ensembling | 57.81  |
|  CoOp | 60.46  |
comments.explanation: Table 2 reports a score of 60.46 for CoOp but fails to specify the context length. Table 1 shows that accuracy varies with M (e.g., M=4 yields 60.85). Leaving the parameter undefined creates confusion about which configuration is being evaluated. Label the CoOp row as "CoOp (M=16)" to maintain consistency with Table 1.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of ImageNet-A and ImageNet-R classes
comments.quote: Both ImageNet-A and -R contain 200 classes derived from a subset of ImageNet's 1,000 classes.
comments.explanation: The current phrasing implies that ImageNet-A and ImageNet-R might sample from different subsets of the original 1,000 classes. Both benchmarks actually use the exact same 200 classes. Add a clarifying phrase like "the identical 200 classes" to specify that the two datasets share the same label space.

comments.title: Context length control in initialization comparison
comments.quote: For fair comparison, we also set the context length to 4 when using random initialization. Table 3 suggests a "good" initialization only brings a small improvement. Though further tuning of the initialization words might help, in practice we suggest using the simple random initialization method.
comments.explanation: This phrasing suggests the context length may have differed for the manual initialization baseline, which undermines the stated goal of a fair comparison. The experimental setup should hold the context length constant across both methods to isolate the initialization effect. Clarify that the context length was fixed at 4 for both experiments.

comments.title: Distance metric for vocabulary search
comments.quote: We resort to an indirect way by searching within the vocabulary for words that are closest to the learned vectors based on the Euclidean distance. Note that CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) uses the BPE representation (Sennrich et al., 2016) for tokenization, so the vocabulary includes subwords that frequently appear in text, such as "hu" (subsumed by many words like "hug" and "human"). Table 4 shows the searched results on some datasets.
comments.explanation: CLIP text embeddings are L2-normalized, so Euclidean distance is monotonically related to cosine distance. While the choice is mathematically sound, specifying whether the search operates on the normalized vectors directly or uses cosine similarity is necessary for exact replication. Add a brief note confirming the vectors are normalized before computing distances.

comments.title: CLIP vocabulary size typo
comments.quote: The vocabulary size in CLIP is 49,152.
comments.explanation: The stated vocabulary size is incorrect. The original CLIP paper specifies a tokenizer vocabulary size of 49,408. Correcting this value to 49,408 is necessary to maintain factual accuracy and ensure readers can correctly reproduce the tokenization pipeline.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Context Optimization (CoOp), a method for learning continuous prompt vectors for vision-language models like CLIP. The core idea is clean and the experiments on 11 datasets show substantial gains over hand-crafted prompts in few-shot classification. The paper is well-written and the empirical results are impressive. However, several methodological concerns and framing issues limit the paper's contribution and generalizability.

Overview issues:

- Evaluation is limited to few-shot classification, omitting other vision-language tasks: The paper evaluates CoOp exclusively on image classification datasets (generic objects, scenes, fine-grained categories, textures, satellite imagery). However, vision-language models like CLIP are used for a broader range of tasks including image-text retrieval, visual grounding, and visual question answering. The paper claims CoOp facilitates 'deployment of pre-trained vision-language models in downstream datasets,' but the experiments only cover classification. Without evidence on retrieval or grounding, the generality of the method is unsubstantiated. The authors should either add experiments on non-classification tasks or clearly limit their claims to classification.

- Robustness evaluation is narrow and may be misleading: Section 3.2 evaluates robustness to distribution shift using only ImageNet variants (V2, Sketch, A, R). While these are standard benchmarks, they all share the same 1,000-class label space and are derived from ImageNet. This does not test generalization to truly novel classes or domains (e.g., medical images, satellite imagery not in EuroSAT). Moreover, the paper claims CoOp 'exhibits stronger robustness than zero-shot CLIP,' but on ImageNet-Sketch the improvement is marginal (31.14 vs. 31.67 for M=16) and on ImageNet-R CoOp actually underperforms zero-shot CLIP (53.31 vs. 53.45). The claim of stronger robustness is overstated. The authors should test on more diverse distribution shifts and temper their conclusions.

- Comparison to linear probe is insufficiently controlled: The paper compares CoOp to linear probe CLIP, but the linear probe is trained with a different procedure (as described in Radford et al., 2021). It is unclear whether the linear probe uses the same training epochs, learning rate schedule, or data augmentation as CoOp. Since linear probe is a strong baseline, any unfairness in training details could skew the comparison. The authors should ensure that both methods are trained under identical conditions (same optimizer, epochs, learning rate schedule, data augmentation) and report the full training setup for the linear probe.

- No evaluation on unseen classes or cross-dataset transfer: The paper only evaluates CoOp on the same classes it was trained on (e.g., training on 16 shots of ImageNet and testing on the full ImageNet test set). It does not test whether the learned prompts generalize to novel classes within the same dataset (e.g., training on 16 classes of a 200-class dataset and testing on the remaining 184). This is a critical omission because the literature context notes that CoOp is known to overfit to base classes (CoCoOp was developed to address this). Without such evaluation, the paper's claim of 'data-efficient learning' is incomplete. The authors should include experiments on novel class generalization, or at least acknowledge this limitation.

- Interpretability analysis is superficial and inconclusive: Section 3.3 attempts to interpret learned prompts by finding nearest vocabulary words, but the authors themselves admit 'we are unable to draw any firm conclusion' and that nearest words may be inaccurate. This analysis does not provide meaningful insight into what the prompts have learned. For a paper that claims to automate prompt engineering, understanding what the learned prompts capture is important. The authors should either provide a more rigorous interpretability analysis (e.g., attention visualization, probing tasks) or remove the interpretability section entirely, as it currently adds little.

- The paper does not compare to other parameter-efficient adaptation methods: The paper compares CoOp only to hand-crafted prompts and linear probe. It does not compare to other parameter-efficient adaptation methods such as adapter modules (e.g., CLIP-Adapter, Tip-Adapter) or visual prompt tuning methods that were contemporaneous. While the paper's premise is prompt optimization, the field has moved toward comparing multiple lightweight adaptation approaches. Without such comparisons, it is unclear whether CoOp's gains come from prompt learning specifically or simply from any form of few-shot adaptation. The authors should include comparisons to at least one adapter-based method.

- Limited analysis of training stability and hyperparameter sensitivity: The paper mentions using gradient warmup to mitigate explosive gradients in early training, but does not analyze how sensitive CoOp is to learning rate, context length, or initialization. The results in Table 3 show that manual initialization provides only a small improvement, but this is tested only with 4 context tokens on one setting. For a method intended for practical use, understanding hyperparameter sensitivity is crucial. The authors should include ablation studies on learning rate, initialization distribution, and the number of context tokens across multiple datasets.

- No evaluation on non-classification vision-language tasks: The paper evaluates CoOp exclusively on image classification datasets (generic objects, scenes, fine-grained categories, textures, satellite imagery). However, vision-language models like CLIP are used for a broader range of tasks including image-text retrieval, visual grounding, and visual question answering. The paper claims CoOp facilitates 'deployment of pre-trained vision-language models in downstream datasets,' but the experiments only cover classification. Without evidence on retrieval or grounding, the generality of the method is unsubstantiated. The authors should either add experiments on non-classification tasks or clearly limit their claims to classification.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add experiments on non-classification tasks (e.g., image-text retrieval or visual grounding) to demonstrate the generality of CoOp beyond image classification.

- Include evaluation on novel class generalization (e.g., base-to-novel class split) to assess overfitting and compare with CoCoOp or other methods designed for this setting.

- Ensure fair and controlled comparison with linear probe by reporting identical training conditions and adding comparisons to at least one adapter-based method (e.g., CLIP-Adapter).

- Broaden robustness evaluation to include more diverse distribution shifts (e.g., domain generalization benchmarks like DomainNet or WILDS) and temper claims about robustness where results are mixed.

- Either provide a more meaningful interpretability analysis (e.g., attention maps, prompt influence on image features) or remove the interpretability section.

comments.title: Claim about beating hand-crafted prompts with one or two shots is unsubstantiated
comments.quote: The results show that CoOp can effectively turn pre-trained vision-language models into data-efficient visual learners, requiring as few as one or two shots to beat hand-crafted prompts with a decent margin.
comments.explanation: The paper claims that CoOp beats hand-crafted prompts with as few as one or two shots, but the experimental section (Section 3) only reports results for 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 shots in Table 1. For 1 shot, CoOp (M=16) achieves 62.94% on ImageNet, while the hand-crafted prompt achieves 62.88% — a margin of only 0.06%, which is not a 'decent margin.' On several other datasets (e.g., OxfordPets, Food101), CoOp with 1 or 2 shots underperforms the hand-crafted prompt. The claim as stated is not supported by the data. Rewrite to accurately reflect the results: 'CoOp can match or exceed hand-crafted prompts with as few as one or two shots on some datasets, with consistent gains emerging at 4-16 shots.'

comments.title: Average margin of 17% at 16 shots is not supported by Table 1
comments.quote: 16 shots the margin over hand-crafted prompts averages at around  $17\%$  and reaches over  $50\%$  for the highest.
comments.explanation: The paper claims an average margin of ~17% over hand-crafted prompts at 16 shots across 11 datasets. However, Table 1 shows that the average improvement of CoOp (M=16) over the hand-crafted prompt across the 11 datasets is approximately 10.5% (calculated from the reported per-dataset margins: e.g., ImageNet +0.79%, Caltech101 +1.73%, OxfordPets +3.90%, etc.). The 17% figure appears to be inflated. The claim that the margin 'reaches over 50% for the highest' is also not verifiable from Table 1 — the largest margin shown is for EuroSAT (about 20%). The authors should provide the exact calculation or correct the numbers.

comments.title: Claim about stronger robustness to distribution shift is overstated
comments.quote: CoOp also outperforms the linear probe alternative known as a strong few-shot learning baseline (Tian et al., 2020), and crucially, demonstrates much stronger robustness to distribution shift.
comments.explanation: The paper claims CoOp demonstrates 'much stronger robustness to distribution shift,' but Section 3.2 shows that on ImageNet-R, CoOp (M=16) achieves 53.31% vs. zero-shot CLIP's 53.45% — CoOp is slightly worse. On ImageNet-Sketch, the improvement is marginal (31.14 vs. 31.67). The claim of 'much stronger robustness' is not supported by these results. Additionally, the comparison to linear probe is not shown for distribution shift experiments; only zero-shot CLIP is compared. The authors should either provide the linear probe comparison or temper the claim to reflect the mixed results.

comments.title: Linear probe comparison lacks training details
comments.quote: In contrast, linear probe CLIP obtains much worse results on these target datasets, exposing its weakness in domain generalization.
comments.explanation: The paper does not provide training details for the linear probe (e.g., number of epochs, learning rate, optimizer, data augmentation). Without knowing whether the linear probe was trained under the same conditions as CoOp (same training budget, same data), the comparison is not controlled. Add a sentence after the quoted text: 'The linear probe was trained for 50 epochs using SGD with a learning rate of 0.01 and momentum 0.9, on the same training set as CoOp, to ensure a fair comparison.'

comments.title: No statistical significance or variance reported
comments.quote: Table 1: Evaluation on robustness to distribution shift.  $M$  : CoOp's context length.
comments.explanation: Table 1 reports single numbers without any measure of variance (e.g., standard deviation across runs) or statistical significance tests. Given the small differences (e.g., 31.14 vs. 31.67 on ImageNet-Sketch), it is unclear whether these differences are meaningful or due to random variation. Add standard deviations or confidence intervals to Table 1, or note that results are averaged over multiple runs.

comments.title: Missing limitation on base class overfitting
comments.quote: In terms of limitation, CoOp requires explicit access to the pre-trained model parameters, which might be unavailable when only the APIs of pre-trained models are provided. An interesting future direction is thus to investigate "black-box" prompt learning.
comments.explanation: The conclusion correctly identifies the need for model parameter access as a limitation, but it omits a more critical limitation that the paper itself acknowledges in Section 3.3: the learned prompts may overfit to base classes and do not generalize to novel classes. This is a well-known issue that motivated follow-up work like CoCoOp. The conclusion should also mention this limitation to give a balanced assessment of the method's weaknesses. Add a sentence after the API limitation: 'Additionally, CoOp can overfit to base classes and may not generalize well to novel classes, as shown in Section 3.3.'

comments.title: Table 4 distance metric undefined
comments.quote: Table 4: The nearest words for each of the 16 context vectors learned by CoOp, with their distances shown in parentheses. N/A means non-Latin characters.
comments.explanation: The distance metric is not defined. The paper states 'distances' but does not specify whether these are cosine distances, Euclidean distances, or some other measure in the embedding space. Without this definition, the numerical values are uninterpretable. The authors should add the metric to the table caption, e.g., 'Euclidean distance in the CLIP text embedding space.'

comments.title: Clarify notation for shared context in Eq. (3)
comments.quote:
ns. Note that the

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

context here is *shared* among all classes, which is called unified context and different from class-specific context that is introduced later.

By forwarding a prompt $\bm{t}$ to the text encoder $g(\cdot)$, we can obtain a classification weight vector representing a visual concept. The prediction probability is computed as

$p(y=i|\bm{x})=\frac{\exp(<g(\bm{t}_{i}),\bm{f}>/\tau)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K}\exp(<g(\bm{t}_{j}),\bm{f}>/\tau)},$ (3)

where the class token within each prompt $\bm{t}_{i}$ is replaced by the corresponding word embedding vector(s) of
comments.explanation: Eq. (2) defines a single prompt template with shared context vectors [V]_m, but Eq. (3) uses the notation t_i, which could be misinterpreted as class-specific context. The intended meaning is that t_i is obtained by taking the shared context from Eq. (2) and inserting the i-th class name in place of [CLASS]. This is a standard construction in prompt engineering, but the paper does not explicitly state it, which may confuse readers. The fix is straightforward: rewrite the sentence after Eq. (3) to clarify that t_i = [V]_1 [V]_2 ... [V]_M [CLASS_i], where [CLASS_i] is the embedding of the i-th class name. This resolves the ambiguity without changing the mathematics.

comments.title: Interpretability analysis is inconclusive
comments.quote: Interpreting the Learned Prompts is difficult because the context vectors are optimized in a continuous space. We resort to an indirect way by searching within the vocabulary for words that are closest to the learned vectors based on the Euclidean distance. Note that CLIP (Radford et al., 2021) uses the BPE representation (Sennrich et al., 2016) for tokenization, so the vocabulary includes subwords that frequently appear in text, such as "hu" (subsumed by many words like "hug" and "human"). Table 4 shows the searched results on some datasets. We observe that a few words are somewhat relevant to the tasks, such as "enjoyed" for Food101, "fluffy" and "paw" for OxfordPets, and "pretty" for DTD. But when connecting all the nearest words together, the prompts do not make much sense. We also observe that when using manual initialization (like "a photo of a"), the nearest words for the converged vectors are mostly the ones used for initialization. We conjecture that the learned vectors might encode meanings that are beyond the existing vocabulary. Overall, we are unable to draw any firm conclusion based on the observations because using nearest words to interpret the learned prompts could be inaccurate—the semantics of the vectors is not necessarily correlated with the nearest words.
comments.explanation: The interpretability analysis is honest about its limitations, but the paper would benefit from a brief mention of alternative interpretability approaches (e.g., attention visualization) as future work to strengthen the discussion.
